organization_information.mission_statement: The Midwest Community Arts & History Center is a nonprofit cultural organization dedicated to preserving, interpreting, and celebrating the diverse artistic and historical heritage of central Illinois. Our mission is to make art, history, and cultural education accessible to all residents—especially youth, seniors, and underrepresented communities—through exhibitions, hands-on programs, school partnerships, and community events. We strive to create a welcoming, inclusive space where local stories are collected and shared, where emerging and established artists can showcase their work, and where residents can connect across generations to better understand our region’s past and shape its future. We prioritize collaboration with neighborhood organizations, public schools, and local historians, and we emphasize practical, skills-based learning in everything from archival preservation and exhibit design to mural painting and oral history documentation.

professional_development.project_title: Building Community-Centered Museum Educators: Intensive Professional Development for Staff and Teaching Artists
professional_development.audience_served: The primary audience for this professional development initiative is our internal and closely affiliated education team, which currently includes 8 full-time staff members (education, public programs, and visitor services), 6 part-time teaching artists, 4 volunteer docents, and 8 partner educators from Springfield District 186 public schools and two nearby community colleges. These individuals work directly with approximately 3,500 K–12 students, 600 college students, and more than 4,000 adult and family visitors each year through field trips, after-school programs, weekend workshops, and community events. The cohort we are focusing on is diverse in both background and experience level: about half identify as early-career educators who have never had formal museum-education training, while others are mid-career teachers and artists who are looking to update their skills around culturally responsive teaching, trauma-informed practice, and digital engagement. Many of our partner teachers serve classrooms where 70–85% of students qualify for free or reduced lunch, with significant populations of Black, Latinx, and immigrant families. Several of our docents are retired educators who are eager to learn how to adapt their long-standing teaching styles to better meet the needs of today’s learners, particularly students with different learning styles, English language learners, and visitors on the autism spectrum. In short, this training is designed for the people who are on the front lines of delivering our mission every day—those who lead tours, design lesson plans, facilitate art-making activities, and represent the Center to first-time visitors who may be stepping into a museum space for the very first time.
professional_development.region_served: The project primarily serves the greater Springfield metropolitan area in central Illinois, including Springfield and surrounding communities such as Chatham, Sherman, Rochester, and Riverton, as well as several rural townships within a roughly 45-mile radius. Our education programs already reach students from multiple school districts across Sangamon County and neighboring counties like Menard and Macoupin. Through this professional development initiative, we are intentionally focusing on strengthening relationships with schools and community organizations that serve low-income neighborhoods on Springfield’s east and north sides, where families often face transportation barriers and have fewer opportunities to participate in structured arts and cultural programs. Additionally, because we frequently host visitors from across the Midwest who come to Springfield for its historic sites, the training will indirectly benefit regional and out-of-state visitors by equipping our educators with stronger interpretive skills, inclusive storytelling approaches, and strategies for connecting local history to broader national themes. While our physical location is in Springfield, the ripple effects of this project will extend to partner organizations and classrooms throughout central Illinois via shared curricula, online resources, and collaborative teacher workshops that we plan to co-host in nearby communities.
professional_development.project_program_description:
This professional development project is an eight-month, multi-phase initiative designed to strengthen the skills, confidence, and collaborative practices of the Midwest Community Arts & History Center’s education staff, teaching artists, docents, and partner educators. The overarching goal is to build a cohesive, community-centered approach to museum education that is inclusive, trauma-informed, and responsive to the lived experiences of the people we serve in central Illinois.

The project has four major components:

1. **Foundations in Community-Centered Museum Education (March–April 2025)**  
During this initial phase, all 26 participants will attend a series of four intensive, three-hour workshops led by an external consultant with extensive experience in museum education and culturally responsive pedagogy. These workshops will cover:
- Core principles of museum education and how they differ from (and complement) traditional classroom teaching.
- Culturally sustaining and anti-bias teaching strategies, with concrete examples of how to apply them in galleries, historic house tours, and hands-on art studios.
- Trauma-informed practices relevant to our local context, including how to support students who may have experienced community violence, housing instability, or intergenerational trauma.
- Techniques for facilitating open-ended dialogue and inquiry-based learning with diverse age groups, including very young children and multi-generational family groups.

Each workshop will include role-playing, small-group discussion, and time for participants to adapt these strategies to specific exhibitions and programs at our Center. For example, one session will focus on rethinking how we interpret a long-standing exhibit about Springfield’s industrial history to better incorporate stories from Black and immigrant workers, and to invite visitors to share their own family histories.

2. **Curriculum & Program Design Lab (May–July 2025)**  
In the second phase, participants will form smaller working groups (e.g., K–5, middle school, high school, adult/family audiences, and accessibility-focused programming) to redesign at least one existing program or develop a new pilot program. Each group will meet twice per month, with guidance from the lead consultant and the Education & Programs Manager (the primary contact on this application). Activities in this phase will include:
- Auditing current lesson plans, tour scripts, and art-making activities to identify outdated content, gaps in representation, or activities that no longer align with our mission and values.
- Incorporating local primary sources (oral histories, photographs, letters, and artifacts) into new and revised programs.
- Building cross-curricular connections with classroom standards, especially in social studies, English language arts, and visual arts.
- Designing clear learning objectives and simple assessment tools (exit tickets, reflection prompts, or quick surveys) that can be used consistently across programs.

For example, one group plans to overhaul our popular “Springfield Stories” fourth-grade tour to include a pre-visit classroom activity that asks students to interview a family member about a meaningful object or tradition, and then connect that personal story to objects they encounter in the museum. Another group will focus on creating a multi-session workshop series for teens that combines historical research, mural design, and digital storytelling.

3. **Peer Observation, Coaching, and Practice (August–October 2025)**  
The third phase centers on real-world practice and feedback. Each participant will:
- Observe at least two programs led by a colleague, using a shared observation rubric that focuses on visitor engagement, inclusive language, pacing, and responsiveness to participant questions.
- Receive at least one one-on-one coaching session from the consultant or the Education & Programs Manager, focused on a specific goal area (for example, improving questioning techniques, managing large groups, or integrating hands-on activities more effectively).
- Lead at least one pilot program or tour that incorporates newly developed or revised content from the Curriculum & Program Design Lab.

We will record several of these sessions (with appropriate permissions) to use as internal training resources and to support reflective practice. Staff and teaching artists have expressed that they rarely have the opportunity to watch each other teach or to receive structured feedback; this phase is intended to normalize peer observation as a supportive, growth-oriented activity rather than an evaluative or punitive one.

4. **Reflection, Documentation, and Sustainability (November 2025)**  
In the final month, the cohort will reconvene for two half-day retreats focused on reflection, documentation, and planning for sustainability. These sessions will include:
- Group reflection on what participants learned, what surprised them, and how their practice has changed.
- Compilation of revised lesson plans, tour outlines, and activity guides into a shared digital handbook that will live on our internal network and be updated annually.
- Development of a simple, ongoing professional development calendar for 2026, including quarterly in-house workshops, at least one external site visit to another museum or cultural organization, and continued peer observation.
- A discussion of how to onboard new staff, teaching artists, and docents using materials and strategies developed through this project.

**Why this project is needed**  
Over the past five years, our organization has grown rapidly, adding new exhibitions, expanding school partnerships, and increasing the number of public programs we offer. However, our investment in structured professional development has not kept pace with this growth. Many of our teaching artists are talented practitioners but have limited formal training in pedagogy, particularly in working with students who have experienced trauma or who are navigating multiple cultural and linguistic identities. At the same time, several of our long-time docents have expressed feeling unsure about how to address sensitive topics like racism, labor exploitation, or immigration in ways that are honest yet age-appropriate.

We have also received direct feedback from teachers and community partners asking for more opportunities to co-create programs and for clearer connections between our offerings and classroom goals. This project is a direct response to those requests. By bringing together staff, artists, docents, and partner educators in a sustained, collaborative learning environment, we aim to create more consistent, high-quality experiences for visitors while honoring the different strengths that each participant brings.

**Intended outcomes and impact**  
By the end of this project, we expect to see:
- At least 10 revised or newly developed programs (including school tours, workshops, and family events) that reflect community-centered, inclusive practices.
- Increased confidence among staff and teaching artists in facilitating dialogue around complex historical and social topics, as measured by pre- and post-project self-assessment surveys.
- Stronger, more reciprocal relationships with at least three key school or community partners, evidenced by co-designed programs, shared planning meetings, and ongoing communication.
- A replicable internal professional development framework (observation tools, reflection prompts, and a digital handbook) that can be used to onboard new team members and support continuous learning.

This project is deliberately structured to be practical and immediately applicable. We are not just attending one-off webinars or conferences; we are embedding learning into our day-to-day practice, co-creating materials that will be used right away, and building habits—like peer observation and reflective planning—that we can sustain long after the grant period ends. Ultimately, the people who will benefit most are the students, families, and community members who walk through our doors and encounter educators who are better prepared to meet them where they are, honor their experiences, and invite them into meaningful conversations about art, history, and their own place in the story of our region.
